en.extract:
The Antioquia brushfinch (Atlapetes blancae) is a poorly known species of bird from Colombia. It was scientifically described in 2007 on basis of three museum specimens from Antioquia, Colombia, which were previously mislabelled as slaty brushfinches (Atlapetus schistaceus). The specific epithet blancae refers to the whitish underparts of the new species, while also commemorating the Colombian lepidopterologist Blanca Huertas, the wife of ornithologist Thomas M. Donegan (who described the species). All three museum skins were collected in the 20th century, but only one label has a date, which is given as 1971. Despite recent research attempts, much of what is known of the species remains preliminary.


Rediscovery
In January 2018, a Colombian resident of San Pedro de los Milagros noticed this bird on his way to Sunday mass. After multiple failed attempts, subsequent fieldwork in Antioquia managed to find the species again. The bird had been previously overlooked despite living relatively close to the city of Medellín, which is home to 3.7 million people. Since then, numerous studies have found the bird within and around several nearby municipalities. However, its population size and distribution were found to be small. More initiatives to explore the region are still necessary to determine if more populations exist, and what territory should be protected.


Taxonomy
Since its rediscovery, its status as a distinct species has been approved by the South American Classification Committee. It was placed in the genus Atlapetes, which belongs to the Passerellidae family. The genus is diverse, and rooted in Central and South America, with many species being found in the Andes. While phylogenies of the genus exist, they were completed before the Antioquia brushfinch was rediscovered in the wild and hence do not include it. However, initial genetic testing suggests that it may be most closely related to the slaty brushfinch (Atlapetes schistaceus).


Description
A. blancae is as a large finch with grey plumage; dark grey feathers on its back and tail, with whitish grey on its breast, stomach and throat. It has a reddish-brown crown, a black mask and a narrow pale line along the side of its face called a moustachial stripe. The bird has nine primaries, with some individuals also displaying a small white patch on their wing called a speculum. Individuals have been reported to weigh an average of 27.91 g (0.98 oz), have a wing length around 71.75 mm (2.82 in), and a tail length of 77.75 mm (3.06 in). Males have whiter chests and throats, more reddish crowns, and are generally larger than females. Specifically, males have larger wings and tails, as well as longer beaks. Compared to adults, juveniles have a browner back and underside, with a yellowish lower bill, a browner crown, and a greyer mask. They also have a larger, more lightly colored gape and are smaller than adults due to their shorter tail. The immature stage is a transitional period between juvenility and adulthood, and typically bears a more opaque version of the adult plumage.


Habitat and Distribution
A. blancae is endemic to the Altiplano de Santa Rosa de Osos, a high elevation plateau in the department of Antioquia, Colombia. The bird has been found at altitudes of roughly 2400 to 2900 meters above sea level. Observation records suggest that the population is split into two disconnected areas; one group is in the north of the plateau, spanning the municipalities of Angostura, Santa Rosa de Osos, Belmira and Yarumal, while a smaller southern cluster lives in Bello and San Pedro de los Milagros.
The species mainly inhabits disturbed or regenerating habitats consisting of low-growing trees, native shrubs, weedy grasses, and overgrown pasture-shrubland mixes. These habitats are often found on acidic, rocky soil, exposed to intense sunlight and fluctuating winds and temperature, as well as small wetland areas that form in depressions of the landscape. The birds also prefer when the shrubs and herbaceous vegetation are present but incompletely cover the terrain (25%-75% groundcover). The species is estimated to have a possible range of roughly 521 km2 to 789 km2. However, only a third of this area is made of suitable habitat, with as little as roughly 14 to 19 km2 representing unbroken and continuous territory. Indeed, the landscape is highly fragmented, mostly due to agricultural and pasture development. For example, in San Pedro de los Milagros, 73% of the land is already converted to grazing spaces.


Behaviour


Vocalizations
Researchers have described a variety of call notes and song phrases of the Antioquia brushfinch, allowing them to identify two types of calls and one song.

The first call was described as a soft (tz…tz) that lasted around 0.05 seconds.
The second call was sharper (tzzzz…tzzzz) and lasted around 0.2 seconds.
The song begins with introductory notes made from call A followed by call B, followed by a fast trill (trzzz…), and then a variable number of final ascending and descending (…zcheui zcheui zcheui…) notes. It lasts around 3.7 seconds and had two variations The first variation has no final notes (ends with the trill), lasting roughly 1.7 seconds. The second variation replaces the introductory notes with many long notes similar to call B, adds more trills, and has fewer final notes. It lasts roughly two seconds.
While the purposes of these songs and calls are yet to be understood, other instances of vocal displays have been associated with specific behaviours. Notably, males are believed to emit many different territorial calls, which often include the mimicry of other birds. Additionally, breeding pairs have been heard vocalizing to cue bouts of foraging behaviour, where the female will break from incubating for a short period of time. Vocal activity occurs most often in the morning.


Diet
The Antioquia brushfinch is an omnivore that feeds on a variety of fruits, seeds, nectar and insects. The birds were observed foraging for invertebrates by stripping both larval and adult insects off plants, as well as pecking in wood to find them. Fruits and seeds were collected from plants such as Berberis medellinensis, Weinmannia pubescens and Vaccinium meridionale. When feeding on nectar, the birds have been observed to tear the flowers off for better access to the nutrients.


Reproduction
Reproductive activity in A. blancae is at its highest in May, though breeding season generally spans from late February to early August. The timing of reproduction coincides with greater day length, and increased precipitation. There are two rainy seasons per year in the region, but only the first triggers reproductive behaviour. Overall, these seasonal patterns are similar to most Atlapetes species.
Observations of nesting are limited, but the species has been documented to build low-to-the-ground, cup-shaped nests using dry plant material. The observed nest was 10 cm wide, 9.5 cm long, 8 cm high, 5 cm deep, and contained two eggs that had white and reddish-brown spots concentrated towards the base. Overall, these characteristics are typical of Atlapetes species.
The male and female appear to have distinct breeding roles, where males guard the nest, emitting territorial calls and the female incubates the eggs. This incubation was reported to last twenty days, which is relatively long compared to other members of the genus.


Conservation
The species is considered critically endangered by the IUCN Red List, with only 405 individuals living in a small, highly fragmented area of 322 hectares. The bird's decline is primarily attributed to habitat loss from agriculture and pastureland expansion. These land-use changes also benefit Shiny Cowbirds, which are known to be brood parasites for other brushfinches. Additionally, the population is distributed in two isolated patches, further increasing the risk of inbreeding. In addition, protected areas in the region have little to no overlap with A. blancae’s range, and do not contain its preferred habitats. However, despite their low population size, recent studies indicate a healthy sex ratio, with a 45% chance of being a male.
Since its rediscovery, researchers have highlighted key targets for conservation action. Specifically, increasing protection for low-growing shrubs and thickets, which are not usually considered important for conservation. Farming techniques should also be remodeled to reduce their effects on the bird, meaning community members and farmers should be included in preservation initiatives. Pastures should incorporate wildlife corridors of low-growing trees and shrubs to increase the connectivity of the brushfinch’s habitat. Additionally, fences could be used to envelope small wetland areas within pastures, to avoid trampling and grazing by livestock.
Currently, two sites in the Santa de Osos municipality and one in the San Pedro de los Milagros municipality are recognized as critical habitats. Both support high A. blancae density and suitable vegetation, making them high priority for conservation. Additionally, researchers have proposed surveys in two strips of land that have good connectivity and ideal habitat conditions. The first is between San Rosa de Osas and Angostura, while the second is at the edge of Bello and San Pedro de los Milagros, and could both contain undiscovered populations.


References


External links
Antioquia Brushfinch Information from DataZone by BirdLife
Antioquia Brushfinch Ebird
Antioquia Brushfinch IUCN redlist
Recognize Atlapetes blancae as a species

The '''Antioquia brushfinch''' ('''''Atlapetes blancae''''') is a poorly known species of bird from Colombia. It was scientifically [[species description|described]] in 2007 on basis of three museum specimens from [[Antioquia Department|Antioquia]], [[Colombia]], which were previously mislabelled as [[slaty brushfinch]]es (''Atlapetus schistaceus'').<ref name=":8" /> The [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] ''blancae'' refers to the whitish underparts of the new species, while also commemorating the Colombian [[lepidopterologist]] {{interlanguage link|Blanca Huertas|de}}, the wife of ornithologist [[:species:Thomas M. Donegan|Thomas M. Donegan]] (who described the species).<ref name=":8" /> All three museum skins were collected in the 20th century, but only one label has a date, which is given as 1971.<ref name=":8" /> Despite recent research attempts, much of what is known of the species remains preliminary.

==Rediscovery==
In January 2018, a Colombian resident of [[San Pedro de los Milagros]] noticed this bird on his way to Sunday mass. After multiple failed attempts, subsequent fieldwork in [[Antioquia Department|Antioquia]] managed to find the species again.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Correa Peña |first=Rodolfo |last2=Chaparro-Herrera |first2=Sergio |last3=Lopera-Salazar |first3=Andrea |last4=Luis Parra |first4=Juan |date=2019 |title=Redescubrimiento del Gorrión-Montés Paisa Atlapetes blancae |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/332913121_Rediscovery_of_the_Antioquia_Brush_Finch_Atlapetes_blancae_Redescubrimiento_del_Gorrion-Montes_Paisa_Atlapetes_blancae |journal=Cotinga |volume=41 |pages=101–108 |via=}}</ref> The bird had been previously overlooked despite living relatively close to the city of [[Medellín]], which is home to 3.7 million people.<ref>{{cite web |title=Species Unseen For 47 Years Rediscovered Near Colombian Town Named For Miracles |url=https://abcbirds.org/article/species-unseen-for-47-years-rediscovered-near-colombian-town-named-for-miracles/ |access-date=13 April 2019 |website=American Bird Conservancy}}</ref> Since then, numerous studies have found the bird within and around several nearby municipalities.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Valencia-C |first=Giovany |last2=David Sánchez-Londoño |first2=Juan |last3=I. Villamizar |first3=Ana |last4=Ángel |first4=Alejandro |date=2019 |title=Una nueva localidad de Atlapetes blancae (Passerellidae, Passeriformes), con comentarios sobre su hábitat |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/345388246_httpasociacioncolombianadeornitologiaorgrevista-ornitologia-colombiana_Ornitologia_Colombiana_Una_nueva_localidad_de_Atlapetes_blancae_Passerellidae_Passeriformes_con_comentarios_sobre_su_habitat_A_ne |journal=Ornitología Colombiana |volume=17: eNB07 |via=}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Díaz-Vallejo |first=Mauricio |last2=Chaparro-Herrera |first2=Sergio |last3=Lopera-Salazar |first3=Andrea |last4=Castaño-Díaz |first4=Michael |last5=Correa |first5=Rodolfo |last6=Parra |first6=Juan L. |date=2023-05-01 |title=Use of acoustic monitoring to estimate occupancy of the Antioquia Brushfinch (Atlapetes blancae), a critically endangered species, in San Pedro de los Milagros, Antioquia |url=https://journal.afonet.org/vol94/iss2/art4/ |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |language=en |volume=94 |issue=2 |doi=10.5751/JFO-00248-940204 |issn=1557-9263|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Chaparro-Herrera |first=Sergio |last2=Gomez |first2=Juan Pablo |date=2025 |title=REPRODUCTIVE PHENOLOGY AND AGE AND SEX SIZE DIMORPHISM IN THE COLOMBIAN ENDEMIC ANTIOQUIA BRUSHFINCH (ATLAPETES BLANCAE) |url=https://www.authorea.com/users/889261/articles/1266720-reproductive-phenology-and-age-and-sex-size-dimorphism-in-the-colombian-endemic-antioquia-brushfinch-atlapetes-blancae?commit=54d6429ccded4e906efdd7fe4ab0d17900db54dd |journal=Authorea |doi=10.22541/au.173890660.02921112/v1|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Velázquez-Tabares |first=Luis D |last2=García-Ospina |first2=Sarai Y |last3=Chaparro-Herrera |first3=Sergio |last4=Ocampo-Peñuela |first4=Natalia |last5=Osorio-Olvera |first5=Luis A |last6=Parra |first6=Juan L |date=2025-06-20 |title=Potential distribution and landscape connectivity of a critically endangered species, Atlapetes blancae (Antioquia Brushfinch): Implications for its conservation |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/advance-article/doi/10.1093/ornithapp/duaf041/8168940 |journal=Ornithological Applications |language=en |doi=10.1093/ornithapp/duaf041 |issn=0010-5422|doi-access=free }}</ref> However, its population size and distribution were found to be small.<ref name=":0" /> More initiatives to explore the region are still necessary to determine if more populations exist, and what territory should be protected.<ref name=":0" />

== Taxonomy ==
Since its rediscovery, its status as a distinct species has been approved by the [[South American Classification Committee]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Proposal (332) to South American Classification Committee; Recognize Atlapetes blancae as a species |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop332.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com |access-date=2025-11-08 |website=www.museum.lsu.edu}}</ref> It was placed in the genus ''[[Atlapetes]]'', which belongs to the [[Passerellidae]] family.<ref name=":8">{{Cite web |last=Donegan |first=Thomas M. |date=2007 |title=A new species of brush finch (Emberizidae: Atlapetes) from the northern Central Andes of Colombia |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259147799_A_new_species_of_brush_finch_Emberizidae_Atlapetes_from_the_northern_Central_Andes_of_Colombia |website=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club. |publisher=127: 255-268}}</ref> The genus is diverse, and rooted in Central and South America, with many species being found in the [[Andes]].<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Chaparro-Herrera |first=Sergio |last2=Gomez |first2=Juan Pablo |last3=Arias |first3=Marcela Restrepo |last4=Ponciano |first4=José Miguel |date=2024 |title=Population estimates and sex ratio of the Critically Endangered Antioquia Brushfinch Atlapetes blancae |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bird-conservation-international/article/abs/population-estimates-and-sex-ratio-of-the-critically-endangered-antioquia-brushfinch-atlapetes-blancae/170108CD83B09EAF6F4D0F2E2F063AFA |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=34 |pages=e7 |doi=10.1017/S0959270923000369 |issn=0959-2709|url-access=subscription }}</ref> While [[phylogenies]] of the genus exist, they were completed before the Antioquia brushfinch was rediscovered in the wild and hence do not include it.<ref name=":5" /> However, initial genetic testing suggests that it may be most closely related to the [[slaty brushfinch]] (''Atlapetes schistaceus).''<ref name=":1" />

== Description ==
''A. blancae'' is as a large finch with grey plumage; dark grey feathers on its back and tail, with whitish grey on its breast, stomach and throat.<ref name=":8" /> It has a reddish-brown crown, a black mask and a narrow pale line along the side of its face called a moustachial stripe.<ref name=":8" /> The bird has nine [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]], with some individuals also displaying a small white patch on their wing called a speculum.<ref name=":8" /> Individuals have been reported to weigh an average of 27.91 g (0.98 oz), have a wing length around 71.75 mm (2.82 in), and a tail length of 77.75 mm (3.06 in).<ref name=":1" /> Males have whiter chests and throats, more reddish crowns,<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last=Chaparro-Herrera |first=Sergio |last2=Lopera-Salazar |first2=Andrea |date=2020 |title=Descripción del nido, huevos y comportamientos reproductivos del Gorrión-montés paisa (Atlapetes blancae) |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/338805662_Descripcion_del_nido_huevos_y_comportamientos_reproductivos_del_Gorrion-montes_paisa_Atlapetes_blancae_Description_of_nest_eggs_and_reproductive_behaviors_of_Antioquia_Brushfinch_Atlapetes_blancae |journal=Ornitología Colombiana |volume=17: eNB08 |via=}}</ref> and are generally larger than females.<ref name=":4" /> Specifically, males have larger wings and tails, as well as longer beaks.<ref name=":4" /> Compared to adults, juveniles have a browner back and underside, with a yellowish lower bill, a browner crown, and a greyer mask.<ref name=":4" /> They also have a larger, more lightly colored [[gape]] and are smaller than adults due to their shorter tail.<ref name=":4" /> The immature stage is a transitional period between juvenility and adulthood, and typically bears a more opaque version of the adult plumage.<ref name=":4" />

== Habitat and Distribution ==
''A. blancae'' is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the Altiplano de Santa Rosa de Osos, a high elevation plateau in the department of [[Antioquia Department|Antioquia]], Colombia.<ref name=":0" /> The bird has been found at altitudes of roughly 2400 to 2900 meters above sea level.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" /> Observation records suggest that the population is split into two disconnected areas; one group is in the north of the plateau, spanning the municipalities of Angostura, Santa Rosa de Osos, [[Belmira]] and [[Yarumal]], while a smaller southern cluster lives in Bello and [[San Pedro de los Milagros]].<ref name=":4" />

The species mainly inhabits disturbed or regenerating habitats consisting of low-growing trees, native shrubs, weedy grasses, and overgrown pasture-shrubland mixes.<ref name=":3" /> These habitats are often found on acidic, rocky soil, exposed to intense sunlight and fluctuating winds and temperature, as well as small wetland areas that form in depressions of the landscape.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" /> The birds also prefer when the shrubs and herbaceous vegetation are present but incompletely cover the terrain (25%-75% groundcover).<ref name=":3" /> The species is estimated to have a possible range of roughly 521 km<sup>2</sup> to 789 km<sup>2</sup>.<ref name=":0" /> However, only a third of this area is made of suitable habitat, with as little as roughly 14 to 19 km<sup>2</sup> representing unbroken and continuous territory.<ref name=":0" /> Indeed, the landscape is highly [[Fragmented habitat|fragmented]], mostly due to agricultural and pasture development.<ref name=":0" /> For example, in San Pedro de los Milagros, 73% of the land is already converted to grazing spaces.<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last=Chaparro-Herrera |first=Sergio |last2=Hernández-Schmidt |first2=Mateo |last3=Lopera-Salazar |first3=Andrea |date=2021-08-31 |title=Notas sobre la dieta y el hábitat del Gorrión-Montés paisa Atlapetes blancae (Passerellidae) |url=https://revistas.udea.edu.co/index.php/actbio/article/view/344051 |journal=Actualidades Biológicas |language=es |volume=43 |issue=115 |pages=1–14 |doi=10.17533/udea.acbi.v43n115a06 |issn=2145-7166|doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalizations ===
Researchers have described a variety of call notes and song phrases of the Antioquia brushfinch, allowing them to identify two types of calls and one song.<ref name=":1" />

* The first call was described as a soft (tz…tz) that lasted around 0.05 seconds.<ref name=":1" />
* The second call was sharper (tzzzz…tzzzz) and lasted around 0.2 seconds.<ref name=":1" />
* The song begins with introductory notes made from call A followed by call B, followed by a fast trill (trzzz…), and then a variable number of final ascending and descending (…zcheui zcheui zcheui…) notes.<ref name=":1" /> It lasts around 3.7 seconds and had two variations<ref name=":1" /> The first variation has no final notes (ends with the trill), lasting roughly 1.7 seconds.<ref name=":1" /> The second variation replaces the introductory notes with many long notes similar to call B, adds more trills, and has fewer final notes.<ref name=":1" /> It lasts roughly two seconds.<ref name=":1" />

While the purposes of these songs and calls are yet to be understood, other instances of vocal displays have been associated with specific behaviours. Notably, males are believed to emit many different territorial calls, which often include the mimicry of other birds.<ref name=":6" /> Additionally, breeding pairs have been heard vocalizing to cue bouts of [[foraging]] behaviour, where the female will break from incubating for a short period of time.<ref name=":6" /> Vocal activity occurs most often in the morning.<ref name=":6" />

=== Diet ===
The Antioquia brushfinch is an omnivore that feeds on a variety of fruits, seeds, nectar and insects.<ref name=":7" /> The birds were observed foraging for invertebrates by stripping both larval and adult insects off plants, as well as pecking in wood to find them.<ref name=":7" /> Fruits and seeds were collected from plants such as [[Berberis]] medellinensis, [[Weinmannia]] pubescens and [[Vaccinium meridionale]].<ref name=":7" /> When feeding on nectar, the birds have been observed to tear the flowers off for better access to the nutrients.<ref name=":7" />

=== Reproduction ===
Reproductive activity in ''A. blancae'' is at its highest in May, though breeding season generally spans from late February to early August.<ref name=":4" /> The timing of reproduction coincides with greater day length, and increased precipitation.<ref name=":4" /> There are two rainy seasons per year in the region, but only the first triggers reproductive behaviour.<ref name=":4" /> Overall, these seasonal patterns are similar to most ''Atlapetes'' species.<ref name=":4" />

Observations of nesting are limited, but the species has been documented to build low-to-the-ground, cup-shaped nests using dry plant material.<ref name=":6" /> The observed nest was 10 cm wide, 9.5 cm long, 8 cm high, 5 cm deep, and contained two eggs that had white and reddish-brown spots concentrated towards the base.<ref name=":6" /> Overall, these characteristics are typical of ''Atlapetes'' species.<ref name=":6" />

The male and female appear to have distinct breeding roles, where males guard the nest, emitting territorial calls and the female incubates the eggs.<ref name=":6" /> This incubation was reported to last twenty days, which is relatively long compared to other members of the genus.<ref name=":6" />

== Conservation ==
The species is considered critically endangered by the IUCN Red List, with only 405 individuals living in a small, highly fragmented area of 322 hectares.<ref name=":5" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=2021-01-18 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Atlapetes blancae |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22735460/181746724 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250506184428/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22735460/181746724 |archive-date=2025-05-06}}</ref> The bird's decline is primarily attributed to habitat loss from agriculture and pastureland expansion.<ref name=":5" /> These land-use changes also benefit Shiny Cowbirds, which are known to be brood parasites for other brushfinches.<ref name=":5" /> Additionally, the population is distributed in two isolated patches, further increasing the risk of inbreeding.<ref name=":5" /> In addition, protected areas in the region have little to no overlap with ''A. blancae’s'' range, and do not contain its preferred habitats.<ref name=":0" /> However, despite their low population size, recent studies indicate a healthy sex ratio, with a 45% chance of being a male.<ref name=":5" />

Since its rediscovery, researchers have highlighted key targets for conservation action. Specifically, increasing protection for low-growing shrubs and thickets, which are not usually considered important for conservation.<ref name=":3" /> Farming techniques should also be remodeled to reduce their effects on the bird, meaning community members and farmers should be included in preservation initiatives.<ref name=":0" /> Pastures should incorporate wildlife corridors of low-growing trees and shrubs to increase the connectivity of the brushfinch’s habitat.<ref name=":3" /> Additionally, fences could be used to envelope small wetland areas within pastures, to avoid trampling and grazing by livestock.<ref name=":0" />

Currently, two sites in the Santa de Osos municipality and one in the San Pedro de los Milagros municipality are recognized as critical habitats.<ref name=":0" /> Both support high ''A. blancae'' density and suitable vegetation, making them high priority for conservation.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, researchers have proposed surveys in two strips of land that have good connectivity and ideal habitat conditions.<ref name=":0" /> The first is between San Rosa de Osas and Angostura, while the second is at the edge of Bello and [[San Pedro de los Milagros]], and could both contain undiscovered populations.<ref name=":0" />
